speaker: interviewer
text: The date is March 19, 1995; survivor is Israel Cappell; interviewer, Jay Straus; city, Rego Park, New York; country, USA; language is English. Today is March 19, 1995. The interview is with Israel Cappell. The interviewer is Jay Strauss, J-A-Y S-T-R-A-U-S. It's taking place in Rego Park, New York, USA. And the language of the interview is English. OK, to start off, could you state your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Israel, I-S-R-A-E-L, Cappell, C-A-P-P-E-L-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- do you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they call me Izzie.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday is May 4, 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your city and country of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Lodz, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. We want to start off maybe by talking a little bit about prewar life. Do you remember the address of the house what-- the house you lived in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In--

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you were-- when you were born, say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not remember the address where I was born, but I remember later, when I was a child. We lived in Lodz, on-- the name of the street was Piotrkowska, number 22. And that was the main thoroughfare in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you live with there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived there with my parents, and my brother, and my sisters. I was the youngest in the family. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was life like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lodz-- I don't remember it that much. I do remember that it was-- there was a very big Jewish population. I think there were 200,000 Jews in Lodz. It's the second largest city in Poland. It was a textile city. Most Jews were employed or were textile merchants who were employed in the textile industry. My father also was a textile merchant. And I remember I was going to the Jewish school, which they called in Poland-- like they call it Yeshiva-- they call it a [NON-ENGLISH] in Poland. I do remember the school. There were a lot of kids. And I was in pre-first, or first grade. And that's about all I remember from-- from Lodz. I-- I do remember certain-- certain scenes which struck me as a child. As I remember, the Jewish boys that were selling bagel, we called them the bagel boys. They are very poor kids. And they were running in the street with the basket of bagel, or bagels, on their arm, where the bagels had larger holes-- should fit over the arm of a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, of course, how much could they make with these bagels? A few cents a day. And they were always pursued by the Polish policemen. That was the main business-- was to pursue these Jewish kids with their bagels.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- they would-- they would catch them. And they would confiscate the bagels, or beat them up and give them a fine. That's something I remember, which now just struck me. I always have the memory. I-- I see the-- the scene happening. Otherwise, I remember a lot of Jews, mostly Hasidic Jews, roaming the streets. And I will say the-- the majority of the Jewish population in Lodz was strictly orthodox. They were wearing different clothes than the non-Jews. They were all dressed in black. They had different hats. They have such round little hats, which they used to call a Yiddish [NON-ENGLISH]-- a Jewish hat. And on-- on Saturday-- Saturday was very much felt in the-- in the streets. The stores were closed. The Hasidic Jews were going with their shtreimel. You know, it was a type of Williamsburg, but magnified much, much bigger. That's the impressions that I have from Lodz. It also was a Jewish cultural city. There was a Jewish theater. And there was a lot of Jewish culture. There was Jewish libraries. There were all kind of Jewish committees. There were all the Jewish organizations-- all the Jewish Movement, the Zionist Movement, the Jabotinsky's Revisionist Movement, the leftist. And it was a very, a very lively Jewish life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how religious was your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family was very religious. I-- my mother's father was a prominent rabbi in Poland. He was a Hasidic rabbi. He was-- they called him the [NON-ENGLISH] Rabbi. And he was known-- he is still known, amongst Hasidim. He was the main composer of Hasidic music. Most of the Hasidic music that you hear was composed by the [NON-ENGLISH] Rabbi-- by him or his son, or his grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a strong sense of community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was. As far as I remember, there was a-- a sense of community. There were many Jewish organizations that helped-- that helped other Jews. There was a strong sense of community.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there any indications when things started getting bad in Germany? Did people take any preparations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I left Poland in 1930. And we moved to Belgium. My father decided to move to Belgium. Even so, we had visas for South Africa and for Belgium. And my father decided to emigrate to Belgium. In-- in Belgium, in 19-- 1933, when Hitler came to power in Germany, things-- we-- we did feel that there's a certain-- there's a certain removed danger coming, like a dark cloud. Because Nazis were spreading all over Europe. And in Belgium-- Belgium was a very liberal country, very democratic country, but a Nazi party was founded in Belgium. And as Hitler grew in Germany, so did the Nazi parties in the other parts of Europe. There were two Nazi parties in Belgium, because the Belgian people consist of two people. There's the Flemish people, who speak Flemish, and the French speaking people, which are called Walloons. The Nazi party of the French speaking people was-- was founded by a-- by a Belgium named Leon Degrelle. And the Flemish party had a different-- different person, which I don't remember the name. But the main was the French speaking Nazi party, which at elections, I think, in 1937 or 1938, had 600,000 votes. And they had a number, a quite substantial number of deputies in the parliament. It was called-- the party was called Rex, R-E-X. This was a-- it-- it was a direct branch of the Nazi Party from Germany, which later on, when the Germans came in, joined the Nazis and were considered by the Belgians as traitors. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make any plans to leave? Did you wanna leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We did not make plans to leave, because my father was not financially in a position to move. Because you had to have money to leave. And there was no place where to go. Jews would not be granted entry into any country easily, and they just were just too busy with daily problems to think about moving. I'm talking the first years-- '33, '34, '35, '36, until 1939. Only a handful of very wealthy diamond dealers in Antwerp moved to-- to the United States, or to England. And besides, no one would imagine anything like what's going to happen in the future. We knew, because in the years from 9-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --from 1933 until the war, you know, Belgium was a neighboring country with Germany. And we had a-- a flood of Jewish refugees coming from Germany into Belgium, all the time. And so we knew of the things that happened in Germany, and we knew the way they carried out pogroms against the Jews. We knew of the persecution. But first of all, we never imagined they would come to Belgium. And-- and then, we never imagined that they would be out there to exterminate the Jew. And in the beginning, everybody thought that the Nazis are a temporary thing, that they will be in power two years, or three years, or four years, and then the German people will overthrow them. Because they wouldn't like the way they're handling the-- the government in the country. So there was no question of making preparation to get out of Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did things start to deteriorate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Things started to deteriorate when the war broke out, when they entered-- the Nazis entered Poland in September, 1939. And the-- France and England declared war on Germany. But Belgium and Holland declared their neutrality. And the-- Hitler guaranteed that neutrality. So we knew-- we-- we believed that there is no danger-- what we are concerned. Now, when Poland was invaded, the-- in Poland, persecutions started immediately. And we received-- since we were neutral, we were in a neutral country, we received lots of letters, which I still have, dozens of letters from the entire family in Poland-- from friends, from cousins, from anybody that knew our name, for help. Each letter, each postcard was an SOS-- help; try to save us; do something; send us food-- I still have those letters. There isn't much we could do at that time. We did send packages of food to Poland, and we did answer the letters. But I don't think that my father was in a position, or anybody else was in a position, to do anything for the people which were trapped in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were the letters sent from? Were they sent from ghettos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the letters were sent from-- from the ghetto in Warsaw, from the ghetto in Lodz. And also, my uncle, my mother's brother, who was the [NON-ENGLISH] Rabbi at the time, he escaped to Vilna. Vilna was Lithuania. Because as you know, when they invaded Poland, they-- they split Poland between Russia. The Russians invaded Poland at the same time. That was the agreement between Ribbentrop and Molotov, that they split Poland. Russians took a chunk. The Germans took most of it. And then they gave a little part to Lithuania. So Vilna became Lithuania. And my uncle escaped to Vilna, where they had a refuge for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So how did things start to deteriorate for you, personally? Did-- did they gradually start introducing restrictions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Belgium, despite that they declared their neutrality, Hitler invaded Belgium on May the 10th, 1940. I remember it was on a Friday. And at 6 o'clock in the morning, I was awakened by-- by anti-aircraft artillery noise and bombing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Germans attacked Belgium. They-- they bombed all of the main-- main cities. And they broke through on the border. And war started very suddenly. It was totally unexpected.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember we lived, at that time, near the railroad station. And we had a balcony. And when they-- as soon as they attacked-- they attacked on Friday-- right away we saw people, mostly Jewish people, going with their bundles towards the train. We watched like this within the next few days, almost the entire population, Jewish population, of Brussels, going to the-- to the trains. This was--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were being forced to the trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was before the Germans arrived in Brussels. You know, the war started on May the 10th. It took them like 10 days to-- to conquer Belgium and Holland. What-- what happened at the time is the-- the trains, there were very few trains running, because they bombed the-- the railroad. And the trains were taken up by the army. There was general mobilization. It was war. Besides the British and the French came in to help the Belgians. They tried to establish a front, some kind of a resistance towards the Germans. Which at the end didn't work, because they were too powerful. And after a few days, we decided to leave Brussels too, because the fear for the Nazis was great already at that time. We knew, but we didn't realize that they're out to exterminate the Jews, but we knew that they are taking Jews to concentration camps. They are beating them up, they kill occasionally. And-- and-- and Jews-- Jews started to flee, to-- to run where we could run. They wanted to run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at the end, when we decided to run, there were no more trains. And what we did is we-- I remember my-- my older brother went out, and he hired a-- a Belgian with a horse and a cart. And we-- we arranged that he would-- we would pay him 300 francs, and he would take us to the French border. And that's what happened. So my parents, my brother, my brother-in-law, my sister, and other sister, brother in law, and a-- and a baby that was just born, that was a few weeks old-- all of us, we took that cart. Because we couldn't fit on the cart, what we did, we put our luggage on the cart, whatever we needed, and-- and the baby and my sister, and we walked. Before you left, did you try to sell anything? Or there wasn't time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no time to sell it. We had to leave. We locked the apartment, and we left. And we walked. We walked. I-- the first night, we slept-- we slept in a-- I slept in a forest. But my-- my parents did get to-- into somebody's house. And he offered them to sit on a chair all night, inside the house. I slept in the forest. And the next morning we walked again. We walked like-- I don't know-- 30 maybe 40 kilometers a day. We walked towards France. It took us-- I don't remember exactly. And we took us like-- it took us, I think, a week to reach the French-- the French border.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had purchased that-- you had a horse pulling the cart?

speaker: interviewee
text: We rented.

speaker: interviewer
text: You rented a horse pulling a cart.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- was the person you rented it from going with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good-- yeah. He was going with us. Yeah. The agreement was he'll bring us to the French border, and then he'll leave us. While we were walking to the French border these few days, the weather was beautiful. And we had no problem from the weather. And we were-- there were hundreds of thousands of people fleeing on the roads. Besides all the Jews, many of the Belgians were fleeing towards France. The-- the Germans were the German Air Force-- was constantly attacking the roads. They were coming down with a dive-- dive bombers. They would machine gun the roads. Occasionally, they would machine gun the refugees. And many people were lying dead in ditches along the-- along the road. So it was-- it was very dangerous to walk on the roads at that time. The further we walked, the closer we-- we felt the front is getting to us, because the Germans were coming faster than we were walking. When we reached the French border, we heard the sound of heavy guns already in the back. We-- we stayed, I remember, we-- when we came to the French border, we didn't have where to sleep. Each night, to sleep was a problem. Somehow, we managed. We found abandoned farms. We broke into the farms, and that's where we stayed overnight. We came to the French border. I always remember this very wonderful person, a very nice person-- Christian-- a Belgian-- who-- we were in a whole group. Besides us, we were-- there were other people that we met on the road that we knew from before. And he, without hesitations, he opened his house to us. And he-- he took us in-- all of us-- it was a group, maybe of more than 20 people. And he was extremely nice. And he told us to feel at home. He fed us. He gave us food-- as much as we wanted. He gave my parents his own bedroom. He was an extremely nice person. And then when we left, when we left his house, he gave us a basket with 200 hard-boiled eggs, so we should have food when-- our plans were to reach--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I don't remember his name. But I do remember my-- my father had an uncle in Poland who was an extremely, generous, nice, good person. We used to call him the [NON-ENGLISH] or uncle [NON-ENGLISH]-- Uncle Reuben. And we named this-- this man, this Belgian, we called him the [NON-ENGLISH], because he was so nice. Anyway, he gave us food. And our plans were to reach the sea-- the sea coast. Because in World War I, when the Germans attacked Belgium, there was a tiny part of Belgium along the sea coast, which they never occupied. And we figured with the French and with the British army that came in to help, they will be able to hold the part of seacoast. And that's where we wanted to reach. But we couldn't, because we were-- we were cut off by the Germans. They reached the ocean before us. And all the refugees, and all the armies that were in between were cut off-- so were the French, the-- the Belgians, the British, and the refugees. We were all cut off. We couldn't-- we couldn't get through anymore. All we could reach-- we reached a-- the big town in northern France-- Lille. We reached Lille. I remember we reached it before-- before the Sabbath. It was on a Friday. We came to--
